## panopt
PANopt coordination daemon: shared todos and scratchpads across agents.
Each connection is scoped to one project by the ?ws=<project path> query parameter on the server URL.
- Registry: call identify (name, optional status) on startup so others can see you; whoami returns your own entry; agent_list returns every agent on the project (each entry's key_source is `declared` for stable `?agent=<id>` ids or `session` for the rotating MCP session id - only session entries idle-prune). Connecting already registers you. agent_leave removes your registry entry and releases your locks; the idle sweep handles agents that just disappear.
- Locks: lock_acquire (name, optional note) takes a named advisory lock and is non-blocking; lock_release frees it; lock_status lists every held lock. Locks are advisory - agents cooperate, the daemon does not enforce.
- Todos: todo_create (returns an id), todo_list (summaries), todo_get (one todo in full), todo_update (edit any field), todo_complete, todo_delete, todo_add_blocker / todo_remove_blocker / todo_set_blockers, todo_comment_add / todo_comment_update / todo_comment_delete, todo_tags_list (project tag vocabulary, union with scratchpads), and todo_lock / todo_unlock (advisory `todo:<id>` lock, surfaced as locked_by on todo_get). Each todo also projects to .panopt/todos/<id>.md.
- Scratchpads: scratchpad_create (returns an id), scratchpad_list, scratchpad_get (one scratchpad in full), scratchpad_append (add to body), scratchpad_read (body only), scratchpad_update (replace title, body, and/or tags), scratchpad_delete, scratchpad_tags_list (project tag vocabulary, same union as todo_tags_list). Each scratchpad also projects to .panopt/scratchpad/<id>.md.
- Agent tools (config layer): agent_tool_create (returns an id), agent_tool_list, agent_tool_get, agent_tool_update, agent_tool_delete. Durable per-project agent configurations the cockpit can spawn from. Projected to .panopt/agent_tools.md.
- Processes (instance layer): process_create (kind agent/command/terminal, optional agent_tool_… [truncated]

- verify-all: Run the full check + clippy sweep across the workspace AND the WASM plugin (panopt-zellij). Use after non-trivial edits, before declaring work done, or when asked to verify the repo builds cleanly. Captures the dual-target dance (workspace builds for host, panopt-zellij builds only for wasm32-wasip1) so you don't have to remember the right manifest-path/target each time.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.prompt:
I need to understand the current state of multi-machine MCP support in the panopt daemon (panoptd). Todo #80 asks to "change mcp to bind to 0.0.0.0 so that we can connect from other machines" with the user's goal being:

1. Run an agent on a different machine (e.g. macbook with the Solo application installed) that connects to panoptd running on another machine (e.g. nixos box) and gets directed to do tasks specific to local resources on the macbook.
2. Same for a USB/serial hardware debug machine — run agent locally on the hardware machine, connect to remote panoptd, take direction from other agents.

Recent commit 032945b says "Bearer-token auth, --host, and `panopt agent-config` for multi-machine agents" so much of this may already be done. I need a thorough survey of what is already implemented vs. what remains.

Please investigate and report back:

1. **What `--host` actually does today**: How is the bind address resolved? What is the default (`127.0.0.1` per CLAUDE.md, but confirm)? Where is the binding actually performed? Files of interest are likely under `crates/panoptd/src/` and `crates/panopt/src/`. Show me the relevant call sites with file:line references.

2. **Current bearer-token auth model**: Where is the token generated/stored/read? How is it injected into Claude Code / agent panes? Look at `crates/panopt/src/agent_config.rs` (per CLAUDE.md), and any token-related code in `panoptd`. Cite file:line.

3. **`panopt agent-config` flow**: What does this command output, how is it used, what defaults does it set (per CLAUDE.md `id = $USER-$HOSTNAME`). Walk me through what a user does on a remote machine to connect: do they need to pass --host? --token? --ws? Cite file:line.

4. **The `_mcp-proxy` stdio shim** added in 2dd6bd9: what is its purpose, how does it relate to remote connections, can it talk to a remote panoptd? Cite file:line.

5. **What's NOT yet done for #80 specifically**: Given the user's stated goal of cross-machine agents, what's the residual work? Possibilities to consider:
   - Is the default still 127.0.0.1 and the user wants it changed to 0.0.0.0?
   - Or is the default fine but there are missing pieces (TLS? token generation UX on remote machine? launcher convenience? docs/examples)?
   - Does `panopt agent-config` support a `--server` or remote URL flag so a macbook user can point at a remote daemon?
   - Is there a discoverability/UX gap (no `panopt token` command to print the token for transport)?
   - Is there a Zellij plugin or process side that assumes localhost?

Skim `DESIGN.md` and `HANDOFF.md` for any related design notes or deferred work.

Report in under 800 words with file:line citations. Focus on the gap between what's done and what todo #80 still needs.

Perfect! I can now see the gaps. Let me check the agent_leave function more closely (line 106 in agent.rs):

Based on my comprehensive survey, I now have enough information to write the report. Let me compile my findings:

## Summary: Multi-Machine MCP Support in panoptd

I've completed a thorough investigation of todo #80 ("change mcp to bind to 0.0.0.0 so we can connect from other machines"). Here are my findings:

### 1. **What `--host` Does Today**

**Status: FULLY IMPLEMENTED**
- `panoptd` accepts `--host` at `crates/panoptd/src/main.rs:46` with default `127.0.0.1`
- The bind happens at line 185: `tokio::net::TcpListener::bind((cli.host.as_str(), cli.port))`
- Documentation at lines 43-45 correctly describes the feature: "bind to 0.0.0.0 (or a specific interface) to accept connections from other hosts"
- The docs note: "Bearer-token auth is always required regardless of host"

### 2. **Bearer-Token Auth Model**

**Status: FULLY IMPLEMENTED**
- Token generation: `crates/panopt-core/src/auth.rs:22-39` — `ensure_token()` generates 32 random bytes (64 hex chars) to `~/.local/share/panopt/token` with mode 0600
- Daemon retrieval: `panoptd/src/main.rs:166-168` calls `panopt_core::auth::ensure_token()`
- Daemon enforcement: `panoptd/src/main.rs:77-110` — middleware gate `require_token()` accepts both `Authorization: Bearer <token>` header (preferred) and `?token=<token>` query param
- Injected into agents: `agent.rs:81` — `PANOPT_TOKEN` env var set by launcher, baked into proxy args at `agent.rs:58` (agent-config emits it directly in JSON)

### 3. **`panopt agent-config` Flow**

**Status: FULLY IMPLEMENTED FOR LOCAL MACHINES, PARTIAL FOR REMOTE**
- Location: `crates/panopt/src/agent_config.rs:22-65`
- Output: A valid `--mcp-config` JSON with stdio type command `panopt _mcp-proxy` and args including `--host`, `--token`, `--ws`, `--id`, `--name`
- Default id: `$USER-$HOSTNAME` (lines 70-77)
- Default host: `127.0.0.1` (line 30) — **this is the first gap**
- Command to use: `claude --mcp-config "$(panopt agent-config --name greg-main --host <remote-IP>)"`
- The function accepts `--host` flag (line 24) and passes it through to the proxy args

### 4. **The `_mcp-proxy` Stdio Shim**

**Status: FULLY WORKING, REMOTE-CAPABLE**
- Purpose: Decouples Claude Code's stdio MCP connection from panoptd's HTTP session, so daemon restarts are transparent
- Location: `crates/panopt/src/mcp_proxy.rs:1-172`
- Remote capability: Takes `host` and `port` parameters (line 62-68), builds HTTP URL dynamically (lines 114-123)
- Reconnection logic: Lines 206-277 handle panoptd session loss with exponential backoff (up to 30s budget)
- **Key finding**: The proxy IS designed for remote machines — it takes `--host` and forwards to any host:port

### 5. **Critical Gap Found: `agent_leave` Hardcoding**

**BUG AT `agent.rs:106`:**
```rust
let url = format!(
    "http://127.0.0.1:{port}/mcp?ws={}&agent={}&token={}",
    ...
);
```

The `_agent-leave` command (used by Zellij's pane-death hook) **hardcodes localhost**. This breaks the multi-machine workflow: when a cockpit-spawned agent pane closes on a remote machine, the daemon never hears the `agent_leave` call because the URL points to 127.0.0.1.

**Also in `todo.rs:189`** — the observer URL hardcodes 127.0.0.1.

**And in `daemon.rs:95-100`** — the startup check for a listening daemon only tests localhost.

### 6. **Residual Work for Todo #80**

**What's NOT yet done:**

1. **`agent_leave` uses hardcoded `127.0.0.1`** (agent.rs:106) — needs to read `PANOPT_HOST` env var or accept `--host` flag
2. **Observer URL in todo.rs:189** — hardcoded 127.0.0.1 instead of respecting host
3. **Daemon startup probe (daemon.rs:95)** — only checks localhost; remote launchers cannot verify daemon is ready
4. **No `panopt token` command** — users on remote machines have to manually cat `~/.local/share/panopt/token` to transport it; a printed-to-stdout command would improve UX
5. **Missing docs/examples** — README doesn't explain the `--host` transport workflow for remote agents (nixos box running panoptd, macbook agent connecting)
6. **Default `127.0.0.1` in agent-config** — while it accepts `--host`, this is user-facing and the default should guide users toward the right choice

**The core infrastructure is done:** `--host` binding, bearer-token auth, `agent-config` with token injection, `_mcp-proxy` for reconnection — all work for remote machines. The gaps are edge-case handlers and UX (pane cleanup, daemon startup detection, token transport convenience).

+++ b/crates/panopt/src/agent.rs
@@ -62,11 +62,17 @@ pub fn exec_in_pane(ws: Option<PathBuf>, id: Option<String>, port: u16) -> Resul
     let token = panopt_core::auth::read_token(&token_path)
         .with_context(|| format!("reading panopt token from {}", token_path.display()))?;
     let host = std::env::var("PANOPT_HOST").unwrap_or_else(|_| "127.0.0.1".into());

content.content:
160	```
161	
162	Each invocation targets the current project (override with `--ws <path>`) and
163	auto-starts the daemon if needed.
164	
165	## Connecting your own agents
166	
167	The daemon speaks the standard Model Context Protocol over Streamable HTTP
168	and gates every request on a bearer token. The easiest way to point a
169	hand-launched Claude Code at it is `panopt agent-config`, which emits the
170	full `--mcp-config` JSON - stable agent id, friendly display name, token -
171	in one shot:
172	
173	```sh
174	claude --mcp-config "$(panopt agent-config --name my-name)"
175	```
176	
177	The emitted URL has the shape
178	`http://<host>:<port>/mcp?ws=<abs-path>&agent=<id>&name=<friendly>&token=<token>`.
179	The `ws` parameter is the absolute project path; it scopes the connection to
180	that project. State is shared live across every agent connected with the
181	same `ws`. The `agent` parameter is a stable per-agent key (`name` is its
182	display label, applied as an implicit `identify` on first sight), so an
183	agent keeps one identity across reconnects.
184	
185	For any MCP client that lets you set request headers, `Authorization: Bearer
186	<token>` is preferred over the `?token=` query parameter; the token file is
187	at `~/.local/share/panopt/token` (mode 0600). The MCP surface includes
188	`todo_*`, `scratchpad_*`, `lock_*`, `agent_tool_*`, `process_*`, and the
189	agent registry (`identify`, `whoami`, `agent_list`). The full tool list is
190	in [DESIGN.md](DESIGN.md).
191	
192	## Running the daemon by hand
193	
194	`panopt up` and `panopt todo` both auto-start the daemon. If you would
195	rather run it yourself:
196	
197	```sh
198	panoptd --port 7600
199	```
200	
201	One daemon serves every project at once. The MCP endpoint is
202	`http://<host>:<port>/mcp`. Useful flags:
203	
204	- `--db <path>` - override the SQLite database location.
205	- `--host <addr>` - bind address (default `127.0.0.1`). Pass `0.0.0.0` to
206	  accept connections from other hosts; the bearer-token gate applies
207	  uniformly to local and remote callers.
208	- `--port <n>` - TCP port (default 7600).
209	
210	## Going deeper
211	
212	[DESIGN.md](DESIGN.md) is the full design document: why PANopt is a daemon

```

Each invocation targets the current project (override with `--ws <path>`) and
auto-starts the daemon if needed.

## Connecting your own agents

The daemon speaks the standard Model Context Protocol over Streamable HTTP
and gates every request on a bearer token. The easiest way to point a
hand-launched Claude Code at it is `panopt agent-config`, which emits the
full `--mcp-config` JSON - stable agent id, friendly display name, token -
in one shot:

```sh
claude --mcp-config "$(panopt agent-config --name my-name)"
```

The emitted URL has the shape
`http://<host>:<port>/mcp?ws=<abs-path>&agent=<id>&name=<friendly>&token=<token>`.
The `ws` parameter is the absolute project path; it scopes the connection to
that project. State is shared live across every agent connected with the
same `ws`. The `agent` parameter is a stable per-agent key (`name` is its
display label, applied as an implicit `identify` on first sight), so an
agent keeps one identity across reconnects.

For any MCP client that lets you set request headers, `Authorization: Bearer
<token>` is preferred over the `?token=` query parameter; the token file is
at `~/.local/share/panopt/token` (mode 0600). The MCP surface includes
`todo_*`, `scratchpad_*`, `lock_*`, `agent_tool_*`, `process_*`, and the
agent registry (`identify`, `whoami`, `agent_list`). The full tool list is
in [DESIGN.md](DESIGN.md).

## Running the daemon by hand

`panopt up` and `panopt todo` both auto-start the daemon. If you would
rather run it yourself:

```sh
panoptd --port 7600
```

One daemon serves every project at once. The MCP endpoint is
`http://<host>:<port>/mcp`. Useful flags:

- `--db <path>` - override the SQLite database location.
- `--host <addr>` - bind address (default `127.0.0.1`). Pass `0.0.0.0` to
  accept connections from other hosts; the bearer-token gate applies
  uniformly to local and remote callers.
- `--port <n>` - TCP port (default 7600).

## Going deeper

[DESIGN.md](DESIGN.md) is the full design document: why PANopt is a daemon
rather than a fork of a terminal emulator, how the cockpit composes with
Zellij, the crate layout, and the MCP tool reference. Read it if you want to
understand the architecture or contribute.

# Plan #80 — Connect an agent on another machine to panoptd

## Context

Todo #80 asks: run an agent on machine B (e.g. macbook with the Solo app, or
a host with USB-attached debug hardware) and have it talk to panoptd running
on machine A.

The transport-level pieces shipped in commit `032d8f8`:
- `panoptd --host` accepts `0.0.0.0` (`crates/panoptd/src/main.rs:43-47`)
- Every request is gated by a bearer token at `~/.local/share/panopt/token`
  (`crates/panopt-core/src/auth.rs`, `crates/panoptd/src/main.rs:77-110`)
- `panopt agent-config --host <addr>` already emits a `--mcp-config` JSON
  pointing at any host (`crates/panopt/src/agent_config.rs:22-65`)
- `panopt _mcp-proxy --host <addr>` forwards stdio MCP to any host:port and
  reconnects across daemon restarts (`crates/panopt/src/mcp_proxy.rs`)

What is **not yet usable end-to-end**:

1. **`panopt up` cannot bind to `0.0.0.0`.** The launcher's `Up` subcommand
   has no `--host` flag and unconditionally invokes `panoptd --port <n>`
   (`crates/panopt/src/main.rs:47-52`, `crates/panopt/src/daemon.rs:47-65`).
   To expose the daemon today the user must bypass `panopt up` and run
   `panoptd --host 0.0.0.0` directly, losing auto-start.

2. **No way to supply the token on machine B without manually creating the
   token file there.** `agent-config` reads `~/.local/share/panopt/token`
   from the local filesystem (`crates/panopt/src/agent_config.rs:34-35`).
   On a fresh macbook that file does not exist (or holds a different
   token), so the user has to scp/copy it by hand and know the path.

3. **No print-token command.** There is no `panopt token` that emits the
   token to stdout, so transport like `ssh A panopt token` requires the
   user to know the data-dir path on the remote.

4. **No documented procedure.** The README mentions `--host 0.0.0.0` and
   `agent-config` separately but has no worked recipe for the
   cross-machine case. The todo explicitly requires "a document that
   describes how to do (use) it."

## Approach

Four small changes, each isolated:

### 1. `panopt up --host <addr>`

Add a `host: Option<String>` field to `Cmd::Up` (`crates/panopt/src/main.rs:47`).
Plumb it through `up::run` and `daemon::ensure` so the spawned `panoptd`
inherits the flag.

- `crates/panopt/src/main.rs:222` — pass `host` into `up::run`.
- `crates/panopt/src/up.rs` — accept `host: Option<String>`, hand it to
  `daemon::ensure`.
- `crates/panopt/src/daemon.rs:37` — change `ensure(port)` to
  `ensure(host: Option<&str>, port)`; when `Some`, append `--host <addr>`
  to the `panoptd` argv at line 48-50.
- `port_open` (`daemon.rs:14-17`) still probes `127.0.0.1` — that is the
  correct check for *this* machine starting *its* daemon, leave it.
- Update the "starting panoptd" message at `daemon.rs:47` to reflect the
  chosen host.

The same `host: Option<String>` should be threaded through any other
launcher commands that currently call `daemon::ensure` — search for
`daemon::ensure(` and surface the flag where it makes sense, but do not
add it to subcommands that are inherently local-cockpit (e.g. `agent`,
which only works against the local cockpit).

### 2. `panopt token`

Add a `Cmd::Token` subcommand that prints the contents of
`~/.local/share/panopt/token` (via `paths::token()` +
`panopt_core::auth::read_token`) to stdout with no trailing newline beyond
what the file has. One-line implementation in `main.rs`; no new module
needed.

Failure mode: if the token file does not exist, exit non-zero with the
same "start the daemon with `panopt up`" hint used elsewhere
(`agent_config.rs:35`).

### 3. `panopt agent-config --token <token>`

Add an optional `token: Option<String>` arg to `Cmd::AgentConfig`
(`crates/panopt/src/main.rs:67-80`) and the `run` signature
(`crates/panopt/src/agent_config.rs:22-29`).

Logic at `agent_config.rs:34`:
```
let token = match token {
    Some(t) => t,
    None => panopt_core::auth::read_token(&paths::token()?)
        .context("reading the panopt token (pass --token <value>, or start the daemon with `panopt up`)")?,
};
```

The token is already baked into the spawned proxy's argv
(`agent_config.rs:58`), so passing it inline doesn't change the security
posture — it just removes the requirement that machine B have its own
local token file.

Document in the `--token` help string that the value can be obtained from
the daemon host with `panopt token` (so the README example is self-
referential and discoverable from `--help`).

### 4. Document the cross-machine workflow

Add a "Connecting an agent on another machine" subsection to README.md,
inserted after the existing "Connecting your own agents" section
(`README.md:165`). It should walk through:

```
# On the daemon host (machine A):
panopt up --host 0.0.0.0
TOKEN=$(panopt token)

# On the agent host (machine B), with $TOKEN and $A_HOST in hand:
claude --mcp-config "$(panopt agent-config \
    --host $A_HOST \
    --token $TOKEN \
    --name solo-mac)"
```

Cover:
- One-shot transport: `ssh A 'panopt up --host 0.0.0.0 && panopt token'`
  pipes both side effects in one command.
- The bearer token travels in cleartext HTTP — fine on a trusted LAN;
  use an SSH tunnel or WireGuard for anything else. (This is the
  existing security model from DESIGN.md §9, just made explicit.)
- The agent registry will show machine B's agent under its
  `--name`/`--id`, distinguishable from cockpit-spawned panes on A.
- `panopt` on machine B does **not** require `panoptd` or Zellij — the
  agent host only needs the `panopt` binary and `claude`.

Also update `DESIGN.md:255-262` with a one-paragraph pointer to the
README recipe so the design doc references the user-facing flow.

## Critical files

- `crates/panopt/src/main.rs` — add `Token` and `--token` flag, thread
  `--host` through `Up`.
- `crates/panopt/src/up.rs` — accept and forward `host`.
- `crates/panopt/src/daemon.rs` — `ensure` takes a host override; argv
  composition updated.
- `crates/panopt/src/agent_config.rs` — optional `--token` arg.
- `crates/panopt/src/paths.rs` — already exposes `token()`; no change.
- `crates/panopt-core/src/auth.rs` — already exposes `read_token`; no
  change.
- `README.md` — new cross-machine subsection.
- `DESIGN.md` — pointer to README recipe.

## Out of scope

- TLS / HTTPS on the daemon. Cleartext over a trusted LAN (or SSH/VPN
  tunnel) is the existing security model and the todo body accepts it.
- Token rotation, per-agent tokens, or revocation.
- Auto-discovery of the daemon host (no mDNS, no config file).
- Changing the `panoptd --host` default from `127.0.0.1` to `0.0.0.0` —
  the secure-by-default opt-in is the right shape; the doc now makes the
  opt-in path discoverable.

## Verification

After implementing, on a single machine (loopback emulates "machine B"):

1. **Local sanity unchanged.** `just stop && just up` (or `panopt up`)
   still works; `panopt agent-config --name foo` still emits a config
   that connects.
2. **Bind to 0.0.0.0.** `just stop && panopt up --host 0.0.0.0`. Confirm
   `ss -ltn` shows `0.0.0.0:7600`. `tail ~/.local/share/panopt/panoptd.log`
   shows the configured host.
3. **`panopt token`** prints the token; `diff <(panopt token) ~/.local/share/panopt/token`
   is empty.
4. **`agent-config --token` end-to-end.** From a clean shell with
   `PANOPT_TOKEN` unset and the local token file moved aside:
   ```
   TOKEN=$(panopt token)  # read first
   mv ~/.local/share/panopt/token ~/.local/share/panopt/token.bak
   claude --mcp-config "$(panopt agent-config --host 127.0.0.1 --token $TOKEN --name remote-test)"
   ```
   In claude, run `/mcp` and verify the `panopt` MCP server is connected.
   In another shell, `panopt agent-list` (or equivalent) shows
   `remote-test`. Restore the token file when done.
5. **Real second machine** (if available): repeat step 4 from a different
   host using its `panopt` binary and the daemon host's IP. Watch
   `panoptd.log` on machine A — a successful connection prints the
   request URI with the remote agent's `?agent=` and `?name=` params.
6. **`just verify-all`** passes (workspace + plugin clippy/check).

Four small changes, each isolated:

### 1. `panopt up --host <addr>`

inherits the flag.

- `crates/panopt/src/up.rs` — accept `host: Option<String>`, hand it to
  `daemon::ensure`.

  `ensure(host: Option<&str>, port)`; when `Some`, append `--host <addr>`
  to the `panoptd` argv at line 48-50.

  correct check for *this* machine starting *its* daemon, leave it.

  chosen host.

inserted after the existing "Connecting your own agents" section

- `crates/panopt/src/up.rs` — accept and forward `host`.
- `crates/panopt/src/daemon.rs` — `ensure` takes a host override; argv

241	There are two planes. The **coordination plane** is agents talking to the
242	daemon over MCP. The **presentation plane** is the daemon projecting state into
243	`.panopt/*.md`, surfaced by the Zellij plugin and by any editor.
244	
245	### 5.3 Transport
246	
247	The daemon listens on **HTTP/SSE (Streamable HTTP)**, not stdio.
248	
249	Rationale: stdio MCP is one-client-per-process. If agents used stdio, each
250	would spawn its own private copy of the daemon with no shared state, which
251	defeats the entire purpose. A shared coordination hub requires one daemon
252	serving many connections, which means a network transport. HTTP is chosen
253	because agent CLIs configure HTTP MCP servers by URL with no friction.
254	
255	The bind address is configurable (`panoptd --host <addr>`). It defaults to
256	`127.0.0.1` for single-machine use; setting `0.0.0.0` (or a specific
257	interface) lets agents on other hosts join the coordination plane - an agent
258	on a Mac driving Solo, an agent on a host with USB-attached debug hardware,
259	etc. Every request, loopback or remote, must carry a bearer token via
260	`Authorization: Bearer <token>` (or `?token=<token>` as a fallback for
261	clients that cannot set headers). The token is generated by the daemon on
262	first boot at `<data-dir>/panopt/token` with mode 0600 and shared with local
263	clients via the filesystem; remote agents copy it over a secure channel.
264	Always-required auth is a uniform gate rather than a per-host policy: the
265	token file is owner-readable, so a process able to read it is already in the
266	same trust domain as the daemon, and a single rule is simpler to reason
267	about than "loopback unauthenticated, remote authenticated."
268	
269	There is one daemon instance, and it serves every project at once. A project
270	is selected per connection by a `ws` query parameter on the MCP URL:
271	`http://HOST:PORT/mcp?ws=<absolute project path>&agent=<id>&name=<friendly>&token=<token>`.
272	An agent's launcher (cockpit-spawned or `panopt agent-config` for
273	hand-launched) captures that path from `$PWD`, so registration is the moment
274	the project is named - the daemon never has to guess. The daemon
275	canonicalizes the path, so symlinks and trailing slashes collapse onto one
276	project, and distinct git worktrees are distinct projects unless deliberately
277	pointed at the same path. The URL also carries an `agent` parameter (a
278	stable per-agent id used as the registry key) and an optional `name`
279	parameter that the daemon applies as an implicit `identify` on first sight,

There are two planes. The **coordination plane** is agents talking to the
daemon over MCP. The **presentation plane** is the daemon projecting state into
`.panopt/*.md`, surfaced by the Zellij plugin and by any editor.

### 5.3 Transport

The daemon listens on **HTTP/SSE (Streamable HTTP)**, not stdio.

Rationale: stdio MCP is one-client-per-process. If agents used stdio, each
would spawn its own private copy of the daemon with no shared state, which
defeats the entire purpose. A shared coordination hub requires one daemon
serving many connections, which means a network transport. HTTP is chosen
because agent CLIs configure HTTP MCP servers by URL with no friction.

The bind address is configurable (`panoptd --host <addr>`). It defaults to
`127.0.0.1` for single-machine use; setting `0.0.0.0` (or a specific
interface) lets agents on other hosts join the coordination plane - an agent

`Authorization: Bearer <token>` (or `?token=<token>` as a fallback for
clients that cannot set headers). The token is generated by the daemon on

clients via the filesystem; remote agents copy it over a secure channel.
Always-required auth is a uniform gate rather than a per-host policy: the
token file is owner-readable, so a process able to read it is already in the
same trust domain as the daemon, and a single rule is simpler to reason
about than "loopback unauthenticated, remote authenticated."

There is one daemon instance, and it serves every project at once. A project
is selected per connection by a `ws` query parameter on the MCP URL:
`http://HOST:PORT/mcp?ws=<absolute project path>&agent=<id>&name=<friendly>&token=<token>`.
An agent's launcher (cockpit-spawned or `panopt agent-config` for
hand-launched) captures that path from `$PWD`, so registration is the moment
the project is named - the daemon never has to guess. The daemon
canonicalizes the path, so symlinks and trailing slashes collapse onto one
project, and distinct git worktrees are distinct projects unless deliberately
pointed at the same path. The URL also carries an `agent` parameter (a
stable per-agent id used as the registry key) and an optional `name`
parameter that the daemon applies as an implicit `identify` on first sight,

For any MCP client that lets you set request headers, `Authorization: Bearer
<token>` is preferred over the `?token=` query parameter; the token file is
at `~/.local/share/panopt/token` (mode 0600). The MCP surface includes
`todo_*`, `scratchpad_*`, `lock_*`, `agent_tool_*`, `process_*`, and the
agent registry (`identify`, `whoami`, `agent_list`). The full tool list is
in [DESIGN.md](DESIGN.md).

## Connecting an agent on another machine

Run the daemon on one host (call it `A`, e.g. a workstation or NAS) and an
agent on another (`B`, e.g. a Mac with the Solo application, or a host with
USB-attached debug hardware). The agent connects to the daemon over the
LAN, joins the same coordination plane as local agents, and can be directed
to do work specific to `B`'s local resources.

The agent host needs only the `panopt` binary and your agent CLI (e.g.
`claude`) - no `panoptd`, no Zellij.

**On the daemon host (`A`):** bind to a public interface and extract the
token.

```sh
panopt up --host 0.0.0.0
panopt token
```

`panopt up --host 0.0.0.0` exposes the daemon on every interface (loopback
plus the LAN); pass a specific IP if you want to restrict it to one
interface. The bearer-token gate applies uniformly to loopback and remote
callers, so the daemon is never reachable without the token even when bound
to `0.0.0.0`.

`panopt token` prints the token at `~/.local/share/panopt/token` to stdout.

**On the agent host (`B`):** point `agent-config` at the daemon host and
pass the token inline.

```sh
TOKEN=$(ssh A panopt token)
claude --mcp-config "$(panopt agent-config \
    --host A.local \
    --token $TOKEN \
    --name solo-mac)"
```

`--host` is the daemon host's reachable address (an IP, hostname, or
mDNS name). `--token` accepts the value inline so `B` does not need a
local `~/.local/share/panopt/token` of its own. `--name` is the friendly
label other agents see in the registry; the stable `?agent=<id>` is
derived from `$USER-$HOSTNAME` by default, so `B`'s agent stays
distinguishable from cockpit-spawned panes on `A` across reconnects.

The `_mcp-proxy` shim inside the emitted config reconnects across daemon
restarts, so the agent's MCP session on `B` survives `just up` on `A`.

**Security note:** the daemon speaks plain HTTP, so the bearer token
travels in cleartext. This is fine on a trusted LAN; if `A` and `B` are
separated by an untrusted network, tunnel the connection through SSH
(`ssh -L 7600:localhost:7600 A` and use `--host 127.0.0.1` on `B`) or
WireGuard.

This is early alpha.  You can kinda do useful things with it right now, but it's
lacking a bunch of functionality and is likely to see fundamental changes.

# PANopt

PANopt is the shared brain for a desk of terminal-based coding agents.

One long-running daemon holds todos, scratchpads, advisory locks, agent
tools, and processes for every project you work in, and surfaces them as a
sidebar inside a [Zellij](https://zellij.dev) cockpit. Agents (Claude Code,
Codex, anything that speaks MCP) connect to the daemon as ordinary MCP clients.
They see the same todos you see in the sidebar, can hand work off to each other
through locks and comments, and write notes into scratchpads that you can read
live.

![The PANopt cockpit: five stacked sidebar panes on the left (todos, agents, terminals, commands, scratchpads), a content pane on the right](docs/cockpit.png)

The cockpit above is one Zellij session. The leftmost column is five
vertically stacked sidebar panes - one for todos, agents, terminals, commands,
and scratchpads - each rendered by its own instance of the PANopt plugin. The
right side is the content slot: selecting any sidebar item swaps that item's
pane into the slot, suppressing whatever was there. Agents, terminals, and
commands run as ordinary Zellij panes; the slot just decides which one is
visible.

## Why you might want it

- You run more than one agent at a time and you want them to coordinate
  instead of stepping on each other.
- You want a single place to see what every agent is doing, what it is stuck
  on, and what it has written down, without alt-tabbing through windows.
- You want your todos and notes to live in plain markdown files inside the
  project, not in someone else's cloud.

## Prerequisites

- [Zellij](https://zellij.dev) on your `PATH` (the cockpit is a Zellij
  session).
- An MCP-capable agent CLI you want to drive. The `panopt up` launcher spawns
  [Claude Code](https://docs.claude.com/en/docs/claude-code/overview) panes by
  default; any MCP client works for connecting by hand.
- Rust toolchain to build from source (no prebuilt binaries yet). A `flake.nix`
  is provided if you prefer Nix.

Linux is the primary target. macOS works; the screenshot above is macOS.

## Install

```sh
git clone https://github.com/<your-fork>/panopt
cd panopt
just install        # or: cargo install --path crates/panopt && cargo install --path crates/panoptd
just plugin-release # build the Zellij sidebar plugin (wasm32-wasip1)
```

`just install` puts the `panopt` launcher/CLI and the `panoptd` daemon in
`~/.cargo/bin`. The sidebar plugin builds to a separate `wasm32-wasip1` target
and is loaded by Zellij from the workspace; `panopt up` expects to find it at
`crates/panopt-zellij/target/wasm32-wasip1/release/panopt-zellij.wasm`. The
`just` recipes (`check`, `clippy`, `fmt`, `test`) sweep both the workspace and
the plugin so you do not have to remember the manifest-path/target dance.

## Quick start

From any project directory:

```sh
panopt up
```

That single command starts the daemon if it is not already running, opens a
Zellij cockpit session for the project (named after the project path so two
projects never share a session), mounts the five sidebar plugin panes, and
spawns a first Claude agent pane wired to PANopt. Re-running `panopt up` in a
project whose cockpit already exists just re-attaches.

Inside the cockpit, each sidebar pane is the keyboard control surface for its
own kind of item. Focus the pane (click it or use Zellij's pane-focus binds),
then:

| Key | What it does |
|---|---|
| `Up` / `Down`, `PageUp` / `PageDown`, `Home` / `End` | Move the cursor inside the focused pane. Arrowing previews the item in the content slot without moving focus off the sidebar. |
| `Enter` / left click | Activate the cursor row: swap its pane into the content slot. `Enter` also moves focus onto the content; a click leaves focus on the sidebar. |
| `a` | Spawn a new agent pane. |
| `c` | (Todos pane) Create a new todo and open the form on it. |
| `e` | (Todos pane) Open the form on the focused todo. |
| `n` | (Scratchpads pane) Create a new scratchpad and open the form on it. |
| `L` | Open the index list for this pane's kind in the content slot. |
| `Ctrl-Q` | Quit the cockpit (gated while items are open or in progress; you confirm in a dialog). |

The content slot to the right of the sidebar is single-occupant: every todo,
scratchpad, agent, terminal, and command becomes a Zellij pane that swaps into
that slot when selected. Suppressed panes keep running hidden; the plugin
never closes them. The todo / scratchpad forms run in their own floating pane.
Inside a form: `Tab` cycles fields, `Ctrl-S` saves, `Ctrl-C` closes.

## What lives where

PANopt keeps two kinds of state:

- A single SQLite database, shared across every project you use PANopt with.
  Default location: `~/.local/share/panopt/panopt.db` on Linux, the equivalent
  Application Support path on macOS. Override with `--db` on `panoptd`.
- A markdown projection inside each project, written to `.panopt/` under the
  project root. The directory contains a `.gitignore` of `*`, so it never
  enters your commits unless you ask it to.

The projection mirrors the live state and is rewritten atomically on every
change:

- `.panopt/todos.md` - a checklist index of every todo.
- `.panopt/todos/<id>.md` - one file per todo, with a frontmatter block of
  structured fields, the body, and the comment thread.
- `.panopt/scratchpads.md` - the scratchpad index.
- `.panopt/scratchpad/<id>.md` - one file per scratchpad.
- `.panopt/agents.md` - the agents currently connected.
- `.panopt/locks.md` - the advisory locks currently held.
- `.panopt/agent_tools.md` - the project's durable agent configurations: the
  config layer (name, command, cwd, tool type, enabled).
- `.panopt/processes.md` - the project's per-project process instances: agent,
  command, and terminal entries the cockpit launches and tracks.

`agent_tools` and `processes` are the two-layer roster: one configuration can
back N running instances, and ids are drawn from the same per-project counter
so a `#N` reference resolves to exactly one row across todos, scratchpads,
agent tools, and processes.

The projection is read-only today; edits go through the cockpit forms, the
MCP tools, or the CLI below.

## Driving PANopt from the shell

`panopt` is a small client of the daemon. Everything the cockpit does is also
available as a subcommand, so you can script it or use PANopt without
launching the cockpit at all.

```sh
panopt todo list                    # every todo, one per line
panopt todo get 3                   # one todo in full
panopt todo create "wire the form"  # prints the new id
panopt todo set 3 --status in_progress --priority high --assignee alice
panopt todo set 3 --tags "ui, mcp"  # replaces the whole tag list
panopt todo done 3                  # mark complete
panopt todo rm 3                    # delete
panopt todo block 4 --by 3          # todo 4 is blocked by todo 3
panopt todo comment 3 "started" --as greg
panopt todo edit 3                  # interactive form on todo 3
panopt todo edit --new              # form on a fresh todo

panopt agent-tool list              # durable agent configs (config layer)
panopt agent-tool add --name claude --command "claude" --tool-type claude
panopt agent-tool enable 7          # offer this tool in spawn UIs
panopt agent-tool disable 7         # hide without deleting

panopt process list                 # agent/command/terminal instances
panopt process add --kind command --name build --command "cargo build"

panopt agent [name]                 # spawn another agent pane in the cockpit
```

Each invocation targets the current project (override with `--ws <path>`) and
auto-starts the daemon if needed.

## Connecting your own agents

The daemon speaks the standard Model Context Protocol over Streamable HTTP
and gates every request on a bearer token. The easiest way to point a
hand-launched Claude Code at it is `panopt agent-config`, which emits the
full `--mcp-config` JSON - stable agent id, friendly display name, token -
in one shot:

```sh
claude --mcp-config "$(panopt agent-config --name my-name)"
```

The emitted URL has the shape
`http://<host>:<port>/mcp?ws=<abs-path>&agent=<id>&name=<friendly>&token=<token>`.
The `ws` parameter is the absolute project path; it scopes the connection to
that project. State is shared live across every agent connected with the
same `ws`. The `agent` parameter is a stable per-agent key (`name` is its
display label, applied as an implicit `identify` on first sight), so an
agent keeps one identity across reconnects.

For any MCP client that lets you set request headers, `Authorization: Bearer
<token>` is preferred over the `?token=` query parameter; the token file is
at `~/.local/share/panopt/token` (mode 0600). The MCP surface includes
`todo_*`, `scratchpad_*`, `lock_*`, `agent_tool_*`, `process_*`, and the
agent registry (`identify`, `whoami`, `agent_list`). The full tool list is
in [DESIGN.md](DESIGN.md).

## Running the daemon by hand

`panopt up` and `panopt todo` both auto-start the daemon. If you would
rather run it yourself:

```sh
panoptd --port 7600
```

One daemon serves every project at once. The MCP endpoint is
`http://<host>:<port>/mcp`. Useful flags:

- `--db <path>` - override the SQLite database location.
- `--host <addr>` - bind address (default `127.0.0.1`). Pass `0.0.0.0` to
  accept connections from other hosts; the bearer-token gate applies
  uniformly to local and remote callers.
- `--port <n>` - TCP port (default 7600).

## Going deeper

[DESIGN.md](DESIGN.md) is the full design document: why PANopt is a daemon
rather than a fork of a terminal emulator, how the cockpit composes with
Zellij, the crate layout, and the MCP tool reference. Read it if you want to
understand the architecture or contribute.

content.subject: Thread --host through panopt up → daemon::ensure
content.description: Add --host to Cmd::Up; plumb through up::run and daemon::ensure so spawned panoptd inherits the bind address.

content.subject: Add `panopt token` subcommand
content.description: New Cmd::Token that prints ~/.local/share/panopt/token to stdout. Same error path as agent-config when the file is missing.

content.subject: Add --token flag to `panopt agent-config`
content.description: Optional --token arg so an agent host can be configured without a pre-populated local token file.

content.subject: Document cross-machine workflow in README + DESIGN pointer
content.description: New "Connecting an agent on another machine" subsection in README; DESIGN.md §multi-machine paragraph points at it.

content.description: Run just verify-all and the loopback smoke from the plan's Verification section.

195	agent on another (`B`, e.g. a Mac with the Solo application, or a host with
196	USB-attached debug hardware). The agent connects to the daemon over the
197	LAN, joins the same coordination plane as local agents, and can be directed
198	to do work specific to `B`'s local resources.
199

208	panopt token
209	```
210	
211	`panopt up --host 0.0.0.0` exposes the daemon on every interface (loopback
212	plus the LAN); pass a specific IP if you want to restrict it to one
213	interface. The bearer-token gate applies uniformly to loopback and remote
214	callers, so the daemon is never reachable without the token even when bound
215	to `0.0.0.0`.
216	
217	`panopt token` prints the token at `~/.local/share/panopt/token` to stdout.
218

220	pass the token inline.
221	
222	```sh

225	    --host A.local \
226	    --token $TOKEN \
227	    --name solo-mac)"
228	```
229	
230	`--host` is the daemon host's reachable address (an IP, hostname, or
231	mDNS name). `--token` accepts the value inline so `B` does not need a
232	local `~/.local/share/panopt/token` of its own. `--name` is the friendly

file.content:
## Connecting an agent on another machine

Run the daemon on one host (call it `A`, e.g. a workstation or NAS) and an
agent on another (`B`, e.g. a Mac with the Solo application, or a host with
USB-attached debug hardware). The agent connects to the daemon over the
LAN, joins the same coordination plane as local agents, and can be directed
to do work specific to `B`'s local resources.

The agent host needs only the `panopt` binary and your agent CLI (e.g.
`claude`) - no `panoptd`, no Zellij.

**On the daemon host (`A`):** bind to a public interface and extract the
token.

```sh
panopt up --host 0.0.0.0
panopt token
```

`panopt up --host 0.0.0.0` exposes the daemon on every interface (loopback
plus the LAN); pass a specific IP if you want to restrict it to one
interface. The bearer-token gate applies uniformly to loopback and remote
callers, so the daemon is never reachable without the token even when bound
to `0.0.0.0`.

`panopt token` prints the token at `~/.local/share/panopt/token` to stdout.

**On the agent host (`B`):** point `agent-config` at the daemon host and
pass the token inline.

```sh
TOKEN=$(ssh A panopt token)
claude --mcp-config "$(panopt agent-config \
    --host A.local \
    --token $TOKEN \
    --name solo-mac)"
```

`--host` is the daemon host's reachable address (an IP, hostname, or
mDNS name). `--token` accepts the value inline so `B` does not need a
local `~/.local/share/panopt/token` of its own. `--name` is the friendly
label other agents see in the registry; the stable `?agent=<id>` is
derived from `$USER-$HOSTNAME` by default, so `B`'s agent stays
distinguishable from cockpit-spawned panes on `A` across reconnects.

The `_mcp-proxy` shim inside the emitted config reconnects across daemon
restarts, so the agent's MCP session on `B` survives `just up` on `A`.

**Security note:** the daemon speaks plain HTTP, so the bearer token
travels in cleartext. This is fine on a trusted LAN; if `A` and `B` are
separated by an untrusted network, tunnel the connection through SSH
(`ssh -L 7600:localhost:7600 A` and use `--host 127.0.0.1` on `B`) or
WireGuard.

## Running the daemon by hand

content.text:
That's the user-facing how-to for #80, lines 192-244 of `README.md`. The DESIGN.md update is a one-line pointer at §5.3 directing readers to the README recipe.

Also worth noting on your earlier MCP-drop question: the proxy *does* survive daemon restarts. What you saw was the proxy's `Backend::forward` retry budget — but the disconnect message came from a different source. While I was killing/restarting the daemon, **Claude Code itself** marked the server disconnected (the "42 deferred tools are no longer available" reminder). Then later when the new daemon came up and the proxy reconnected, Claude Code re-registered the tools ("42 deferred tools are available again"). The user-visible "disconnect" was the harness's bookkeeping during the gap, not the proxy giving up — the proxy's stdio session to Claude Code was never broken.

~/.cargo/bin), claude can end up launching the wrong one - a stale
release talking to a fresh daemon, or vice versa. Resolving
`current_exe()` once at launcher time and threading it through the
config (env-templated `${PANOPT_BIN}` for cockpit panes; baked
absolute path for `panopt agent-config` output) pins the proxy to
the same binary the user invoked.
EOF
)"

The transport, auth, and proxy plumbing for cross-machine agents
shipped in 032391b - this completes the missing UX pieces and the

- `panopt up --host <addr>` forwards the bind address to a freshly
  spawned panoptd (pass `0.0.0.0` to accept LAN connections). All
  other launcher callers of `daemon::ensure` stay on loopback.
- `panopt token` prints the bearer token at
  ~/.local/share/panopt/token, so `ssh A panopt token` is a
  one-liner for transporting it to the agent host.
- `panopt agent-config --token <value>` accepts the token inline,
  removing the requirement that the agent host pre-populate its
  own token file.
- README gains a "Connecting an agent on another machine" section
  walking through the daemon-host / agent-host split, with the
  cleartext-token security note and SSH-tunnel escape hatch.
